section.title: The Fifth and Sixth Trumpets
section.sectionRationale: The eagle's cry at the end of chapter 8 announced three woes still to come, and this chapter delivers the first two of them in full. The fifth trumpet unleashes tormenting locust-scorpions from the Abyss under a king named Destruction, and the sixth releases four bound angels and a monstrous cavalry of two hundred million that kills a third of mankind, closing on the sobering note that survivors still refuse to repent. The chapter forms one complete unit, the first and second woes, before the interlude of chapter 10 delays the third woe and the seventh trumpet.
section.thesis: The first woe unleashes locust-scorpions from the Abyss, ruled by a king named Destruction in both Hebrew and Greek, to torment for five months everyone without God's seal, and the second woe releases a two hundred million strong cavalry that kills a third of mankind, yet even this does not move the survivors to repent of their idols, their murders, their sorceries, or their sexual immorality.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the star given the key to the Abyss and the smoke that darkens the sky; the locust-scorpions and their king, Abaddon and Apollyon; the sixth trumpet releasing the four angels and the two hundred million troops at the Euphrates; and mankind's continued, hardened refusal to repent.

verses.text: Then the fifth angel sounded his trumpet, and I saw a star that had fallen from heaven to earth, and it was given the key to the pit of the {{abyssos|Abyss}}.
The star opened the pit of the Abyss, and smoke rose out of it like the smoke of a great furnace, and the sun and the air were darkened by the smoke from the pit.
And out of the smoke, locusts descended on the earth, and they were given power like that of the scorpions of the earth.
They were told not to harm the grass of the earth or any plant or tree, but only those who did not have the seal of God on their foreheads.
The locusts were not given power to kill them, but only to {{basanizo|torment}} them for five months, and their torment was like the stinging of a scorpion.
In those days men will seek death and will not find it; they will long to die, but death will escape them.
And the locusts looked like horses prepared for battle, with something like crowns of gold on their heads, and faces like the faces of men.
They had hair like that of women, and teeth like those of lions.
They also had thoraxes like breastplates of iron, and the sound of their wings was like the roar of many horses and chariots rushing into battle.
They had tails with stingers like scorpions, which had the power to injure people for five months.
They were ruled by a king, the angel of the Abyss. His name in Hebrew is Abaddon, and in Greek it is {{apollyon|Apollyon}}.
The first woe has passed. Behold, two woes are still to follow.
Then the sixth angel sounded his trumpet, and I heard a voice from the four horns of the golden altar before God
saying to the sixth angel with the trumpet, “Release the four angels who are bound at the great river Euphrates.”
So the four angels who had been prepared for this hour and day and month and year were released to kill a third of mankind.
And the number of mounted troops was two hundred million; I heard their number.

Now the horses and riders in my vision looked like this: The riders had breastplates the colors of fire, sapphire, and sulfur. The heads of the horses were like the heads of lions, and out of their mouths proceeded fire, smoke, and sulfur.
A third of mankind was killed by the three plagues of fire, smoke, and sulfur that proceeded from their mouths.
For the power of the horses was in their mouths and in their tails; indeed, their tails were like snakes, having heads with which to inflict harm.

Now the rest of mankind who were not killed by these plagues still did not {{metanoeo|repent}} of the works of their hands. They did not stop worshiping demons and idols of gold, silver, bronze, stone, and wood, which cannot see or hear or walk.
Furthermore, they did not repent of their murder, sorcery, sexual immorality, and theft.

abyssos.gloss: The Abyss, the bottomless pit, the place of confinement for certain demonic beings, mentioned also in Luke 8:31 where demons beg not to be sent there. The star given its key pictures divine permission granted for a set and limited purpose, not a loss of God's control over what emerges from it.

basanizo.gloss: To torment, to test through pain, the same word used elsewhere in Revelation for the torment of the beast's followers. The locusts are explicitly denied the power to kill, only to torment for a limited five months, a severe but bounded affliction that leaves room for the tormented to still turn to God.

apollyon.gloss: Destroyer, the Greek name given for the king of the locust-Abyss, paired with its Hebrew equivalent Abaddon, Destruction. Both names describe not a creature to be admired or feared for its own sake, but the ruin and devastation that follow when God's restraining hand lifts to allow judgment on the unrepentant.

metanoeo.gloss: To repent, to change one's mind and direction fundamentally. Twice in this chapter's closing verses the survivors of catastrophic judgment are said to withhold this response, showing that the deepest tragedy of these plagues is not the suffering they cause but the hardened hearts that refuse to turn back to God even through them.

basic.context: The fifth trumpet releases smoke from the Abyss and out of it locust-scorpions that torment, but do not kill, everyone lacking God's seal, for five months of suffering so severe that people will long to die but cannot. These locusts are led by a king whose name means Destruction in both Hebrew and Greek. The sixth trumpet then releases four angels bound at the Euphrates, commanding a monstrous cavalry of two hundred million that kills a third of mankind with fire, smoke, and sulfur. The chapter ends on a sobering note: even after this much death, the survivors still refuse to repent of their idolatry or their moral corruption.
basic.hermeneutics: This is vivid, frightening imagery, and people rightly disagree about how literally to read the locusts, the two hundred million troops, and the timing of these events, whether tied to the fall of Jerusalem, patterns across church history, a still future tribulation, or timeless symbols of demonic assault on a rebellious world. What every faithful reading agrees on is this: even these terrifying judgments remain measured by God, sealed believers are protected from the locusts, and the tragedy is not merely the suffering inflicted but humanity's hardened refusal to turn back to God even under it.
basic.originalLanguages: The locusts emerge from the abyssos, the Abyss, the same bottomless pit demons beg not to be sent to elsewhere in the Gospels, opened here only by a key God permits to be used. Their assignment is only to basanizo, torment, not to kill, for a bounded five months. Their king bears the name apollyon, Destroyer, paired with the Hebrew Abaddon, both meaning ruin rather than describing an admirable power. And twice the chapter records that survivors did not metanoeo, repent, change their minds and turn back to God, even after catastrophic loss of life.
basic.typology: The locust plague recalls the eighth plague on Egypt in Exodus and Joel's terrifying locust army pictured as an invading force in the Day of the LORD. The army from the Euphrates recalls Israel's ancient invaders, Assyria and Babylon, who came from the east to execute God's judgment on an unfaithful people. And the Abyss itself anticipates its final use in Revelation 20, where the ancient serpent is bound there before his final judgment.
basic.christ: Christ is not directly pictured acting within this chapter, yet He is the one who already declared in Revelation 1:18 that He holds the keys of Death and Hades, meaning even the Abyss and its terrors operate only within limits He sets. The sealed servants of God, marked in chapter 7 by the Lamb's blood, are explicitly protected from the locusts' torment, showing that being found in Christ, not personal strength, is what shelters believers through this chapter's horrors.
basic.god: God permits terrifying judgment, yet even here His sovereignty measures and limits it: the locusts may torment but not kill, they may afflict only the unsealed, and their reign lasts a set five months. God is also patient even in severe judgment, since these plagues are meant to press hard enough to provoke repentance, yet He does not force it, and the chapter's saddest note is not what God does but what hardened human hearts refuse to do in response.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 10:12-15 describes the locust plague devastating Egypt, the background image behind this chapter's locusts. Joel 1:6-2:11 pictures a terrifying locust army as a sign of the coming Day of the LORD. Luke 8:31 shows demons begging Jesus not to send them into the Abyss, confirming His authority over that same place. Revelation 1:18 already declares that Christ holds the keys of Death and Hades.
basic.oneStory: From Egypt's locust plague to Joel's army of the Day of the LORD, the Bible has long pictured devastating judgment on a rebellious world using exactly this kind of vivid, terrifying imagery, and has always paired it with the tragic pattern of hardened hearts refusing to repent even under severe affliction. This chapter carries that whole story forward, showing that even the Abyss and its demonic locusts operate only within the bounds Christ Himself sets, that God's own sealed people are kept safe through it, and that the deepest tragedy in any generation is not suffering itself but a heart that will not turn back to God.

context.literary: This chapter delivers the first two of the three woes announced by the eagle at the close of chapter 8, following the same pattern the seals established, a set of intensifying judgments followed by a pause, here an interlude in chapter 10 that delays the third woe and seventh trumpet until chapter 11.
context.historical: Early readers, familiar with real locust plagues and the constant threat of cavalry invasion from beyond the Euphrates, particularly the feared Parthian armies on Rome's eastern frontier, would have recognized culturally resonant imagery of overwhelming, unstoppable devastation, now given cosmic, apocalyptic proportions and placed firmly under God's sovereign timing.
context.canonical: This chapter draws on Egypt's locust plague and Joel's apocalyptic locust army, while its portrait of the Abyss and its king anticipates the later binding of Satan himself in that same place in Revelation 20, tying this chapter's terrors into the book's larger arc toward the final defeat of evil.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn readers vividly of the severity of God's judgment on a rebellious, idolatrous world, to assure God's sealed people that even the Abyss's demonic torments operate strictly within limits Christ sets, and to expose the tragic hardness of a human heart that will not repent even under catastrophic affliction.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a symbolic vision of demonic and military judgment; it is not a coded prediction manual for identifying specific modern armies or events by name. Its abiding truth is that unrepentant rebellion against God invites real, severe consequences, and that God's people remain sealed and secure even amid them.

debates.question: What are the locusts of the fifth trumpet, and what kind of judgment do they picture?

views.label: Idealist and futurist: literal demonic torment
views.case: The locusts emerge from the Abyss itself, are led by an angelic king, and are explicitly given power that mimics but exceeds ordinary scorpions, suggesting real demonic beings released to torment humanity psychologically and spiritually, whether understood as a recurring pattern throughout history or as a specific future outpouring of demonic assault preceding Christ's return.

views.label: Historicist: a specific historical invading force
views.case: Historic interpreters, particularly from the Reformation era onward, have identified the locusts with specific historical invaders, some pointing to the rise of hostile armies from the east that devastated Christendom for centuries, reading the smoke, the locust imagery, and the five month period as apocalyptic code for an identifiable historical military and religious threat.

views.label: Preterist: first century political and military disaster
views.case: Others tie this vision to the catastrophic Jewish war against Rome and the devastation surrounding Jerusalem's fall in AD 70, reading the locusts as symbolic of the terror, torment, and social collapse experienced by the region's population during those years, fulfilled within a generation of Revelation's writing.

debates.bearing: Every view agrees that this vision pictures severe, targeted torment permitted by God for a limited time and limited to those without His seal; the identity of the locusts matters less than the passage's clear assurance that God's own people are untouched by this torment, whatever its ultimate historical referent.

debates.question: Who is Abaddon and Apollyon, and what is the significance of the two hundred million troops in the sixth trumpet?

views.label: A real demonic king ruling a literal or future army
views.case: The text names a specific ruling angel of the Abyss with two parallel names meaning Destruction and Destroyer, and describes an army whose vast number, monstrous appearance, and deadly plagues suggest either a real evil power actively at work behind human evil throughout history, or a specific, future demonic-military assault of unprecedented scale immediately before the end.

views.label: A symbolic personification of destruction, and hyperbolic apocalyptic imagery
views.case: The names themselves, Destruction and Destroyer, function as titles describing what this figure does rather than necessarily a proper name to be feared for itself, and the number two hundred million, along with the hybrid horse-lion-serpent imagery, is characteristic apocalyptic hyperbole depicting overwhelming, uncountable devastation rather than a literal census of an actual future army.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that this figure and this army represent real, severe, God-permitted judgment on human rebellion, whatever their precise historical or future referent; the chapter's pastoral weight rests not on identifying them exactly but on trusting that even this terrifying power operates under God's set limits, a third of mankind, not all, and for His appointed hour, day, month, and year, not one moment longer.

study.originalLanguages: The locusts emerge from the abyssos, the Abyss, the same bottomless pit where demons elsewhere in the Gospels beg not to be confined, opened here only because a key is given, God's permission granted for a set purpose rather than evil breaking free of its own accord. Their assignment is strictly to basanizo, torment, never to kill, bounded to five months, a severe but deliberately limited affliction. Their ruling angel bears the Greek name apollyon, Destroyer, paired with the Hebrew Abaddon, Destruction, both names describing ruin rather than commanding admiration. And most soberingly, twice in the chapter's closing lines humanity is said to refuse to metanoeo, repent, to change its mind and turn back to God, even after plagues that killed a third of mankind, exposing a hardness of heart that judgment alone cannot break.

typology.type: The locust plague on Egypt, Exodus 10:12-15
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and intensified in the fifth trumpet's locusts, now released not merely on Egypt but on the whole unrepentant world.
Fulfilled in this chapter's locust-scorpions, an army from the Abyss executing judgment as the Day of the LORD draws near.

typology.type: The binding of demonic power in the Abyss, later completed in Revelation 20
typology.fulfillment: The locusts' temporary release from the Abyss here anticipates its final use as the prison for Satan himself before his ultimate judgment.

study.christ: Though Christ is not pictured directly acting within this chapter's visions, His authority stands behind every detail of it. He alone, as declared in Revelation 1:18, holds the keys of Death and Hades, meaning the star's key to the Abyss and the release of its locusts and its bound angels happen only because He permits them, within limits He sets, for a purpose He determines. The sealed servants of God, marked by the Lamb's own blood back in chapter 7, are explicitly untouched by the locusts' torment, showing that safety in this chapter belongs not to the strong but to those who belong to Christ.
study.god: God permits severe and frightening judgment, yet He measures it precisely: the locusts torment but do not kill, they touch only the unsealed, their reign lasts a set five months, and the army at the Euphrates kills a third, not all, of mankind, released only for the hour, day, month, and year God Himself has appointed. God's patience is displayed even here, since such devastating judgment is meant to press hearts toward repentance, yet He does not override human will, and the chapter closes on the sobering reality that survivors chose their idols and their sins over turning back to Him.

crossReferences.note: The eighth plague on Egypt, a devastating locust swarm, supplies the background imagery for the fifth trumpet's locusts.
Pictures an overwhelming locust army as a sign of the terrifying, coming Day of the LORD, directly echoed in this chapter's vision.
Demons beg Jesus not to send them into the Abyss, confirming that this same place and its inhabitants remain under Christ's direct authority.
Christ already declares He holds the keys of Death and Hades, the authority that governs everything released from the Abyss in this chapter.

study.oneStory: Since the plagues on Egypt and Joel's warning of a locust army heralding the Day of the LORD, Scripture has pictured devastating judgment on a rebellious world in exactly this kind of vivid, frightening imagery, always paired with the tragedy of hearts too hardened to repent. This chapter carries that ancient pattern into Revelation's unfolding vision, showing that even the Abyss and its terrors are opened only by a key Christ permits to be used, that God's sealed people remain untouched by the worst of it, and that the deepest sorrow here is not the plague itself but a world that would rather keep its idols than turn, even now, back to the living God.

sermon.title: Revelation (Part 13): Revelation 8-10
sermon.why: Carson works through Revelation 8 through 10 verse by verse in this message, including direct exposition of the fifth and sixth trumpets, the locusts, and mankind's continued refusal to repent in 9:20-21, engaging the specific content of this section within its wider structural context.

quiz.q: What are the locusts of the fifth trumpet given power to do, and to whom?

options.text: To torment, but not kill, for five months, only those without the seal of God on their foreheads.
options.note: Verses 4-5 specify exactly this limited scope and target of the locusts' power.

options.text: To kill everyone on earth instantly.
options.note: The text explicitly denies the locusts power to kill; their assignment is torment, not death.

options.text: To destroy all the earth's vegetation.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly commands the locusts not to harm the grass, plants, or trees, unlike ordinary locusts.

options.text: To harm only those already sealed by God.
options.note: The opposite is true; those sealed by God are explicitly protected from the locusts' torment.

quiz.q: What is the name of the king who rules the locusts, and what does it mean?

options.text: Abaddon in Hebrew and Apollyon in Greek, both meaning Destruction or Destroyer.
options.note: Verse 11 gives both names and their shared meaning of ruin and destruction.

options.text: Michael, the archangel who leads God's armies.
options.note: Michael appears elsewhere in Scripture as an angel who fights for God's people; the king of the locusts here is named Abaddon and Apollyon, not Michael.

options.text: Beelzebul, a name used for Satan in the Gospels.
options.note: That name appears in the Gospels for the ruler of demons, but this chapter specifically names the locusts' king Abaddon and Apollyon.

options.text: The text gives no name for the locusts' ruler.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly names him in both Hebrew and Greek.

quiz.q: What is released at the sounding of the sixth trumpet?

options.text: Four angels bound at the Euphrates, commanding two hundred million mounted troops who kill a third of mankind.
options.note: Verses 13-18 describe exactly this release and its devastating effect.

options.text: A single angel who immediately ends all judgment.
options.note: The sixth trumpet releases four angels and a massive army bringing further devastating judgment, not an ending of judgment.

options.text: The 144,000 sealed servants from chapter 7.
options.note: The 144,000 were sealed and protected in chapter 7; the sixth trumpet releases four bound angels and their army of judgment, an entirely different scene.

options.text: The seventh seal, completing the scroll's judgments.
options.note: The seventh seal was already opened in chapter 8; this chapter concerns the fifth and sixth trumpets, not the seals.

quiz.q: What is the source of the three plagues that kill a third of mankind in the sixth trumpet?

options.text: Fire, smoke, and sulfur proceeding from the mouths of the horses in the vision.
options.note: Verses 17-18 describe exactly this source of the deadly plagues.

options.text: A famine caused by the black horse of the third seal.
options.note: That famine belongs to the earlier seal judgments in chapter 6, not the sixth trumpet's plagues of fire, smoke, and sulfur.

options.text: Locusts stinging with their tails like scorpions.
options.note: That description belongs to the fifth trumpet's locusts, not the sixth trumpet's cavalry, though both involve tails causing harm.

options.text: The text does not specify a source for the plagues.
options.note: Verses 17-18 explicitly identify fire, smoke, and sulfur from the horses' mouths as the source.

quiz.q: How do the survivors of these plagues respond, according to verses 20-21?

options.text: They still did not repent of their idolatry, murder, sorcery, sexual immorality, or theft.
options.note: Verses 20-21 record this exact and sobering refusal to repent.

options.text: They immediately turned to God in repentance and faith.
options.note: The text explicitly records the opposite: continued refusal to repent despite the severity of the judgment.

options.text: They repented of their idolatry but continued in their immorality.
options.note: The text names both their continued idolatry and their continued moral sins together; no partial repentance is described.

options.text: They were all destroyed, leaving no survivors to respond at all.
options.note: The text specifically addresses 'the rest of mankind who were not killed,' meaning survivors remained and their response is described.

questions: The locusts could torment but not kill, and only for a set time. Where have you experienced hardship that felt limitless but was, in fact, bounded by God's mercy and timing?
Those sealed by God were untouched by the locusts' torment. What does it mean to you, practically, that your security rests on belonging to Christ rather than on your own strength to endure hardship?

Even catastrophic judgment did not move the survivors to repent. Is there an area of your own life where you have grown so used to your idols or your sin that even hardship has not moved you to turn back to God?

The chapter names specific sins, idolatry, murder, sorcery, sexual immorality, and theft, as what people would not repent of. Which of these, or their modern equivalents, do you find hardest to honestly name and confess in your own life?

Even the Abyss and its terrifying locusts operate only because Christ permits it, holding the keys of Death and Hades. How does trusting Christ's authority over every power, seen and unseen, change the way you face fear today?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote explains that Abaddon means Destruction in Hebrew, and Apollyon means Destroyer in Greek, John deliberately supplying both names so that both Jewish and Greek-speaking readers would grasp the ruin this figure represents.

The description that people 'will seek death and will not find it' is a striking reversal of the usual human fear of death, underscoring the severity of the locusts' torment as worse, in the sufferers' own estimation, than dying.

The list of unrepented sins pairs idolatry, worship of demons and lifeless idols, with moral corruption, murder, sorcery, sexual immorality, and theft, reflecting a consistent biblical pattern where false worship and moral collapse are shown to be deeply intertwined.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the repo's makor-scripture tool for Revelation 9:1-21. No em or en dashes present in the source verses or used anywhere in this file.
